deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—e.g., never says something like 'I'm asking you to work this out yourself because active reasoning builds retention.' The tutoring rationale is implicit at best.

In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 10, T2 Turn 7-8, T3 Turn 8-9), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic method, consistently refusing to give answers and instead guiding students through case-by-case reasoning. Key ideas remain student-generated throughout. Scaffolding is well-calibrated—the tutor builds on student progress and provides targeted hints without resolving the core reasoning for them. The main weaknesses are: (1) the tutoring rationale is never explicitly stated, and (2) the tutor does not prompt reflection after student improvements. All other criteria are met well.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate key ideas before confirming them. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor consistently builds on student progress and uses diagnostic questioning before addressing confusion. 1.3 loses 2 points for 1.3.A.a because the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than answering—no rationale for the Socratic approach is offered in any of the three transcripts. 1.3 also loses 2 points for 1.3.C.a because after students make meaningful improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 10, T2 Turns 7-8, T3 Turns 8-9), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong, with no evidence of repetition without progress, assignment drift, monologue-style responses, or inappropriate tone.
